message.content:
You are an INDEPENDENT verifier adjudicating audit finding GitHub issue #12 for /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh. Run repros with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.

FINDING (#12, stated severity LOW): Every adapter contract (parser.py, claims.py, features.py, baseline/toolkit.py) is a @runtime_checkable Protocol; isinstance against these checks only attribute presence, not callability/signature, so isinstance(x, IngestionParser) is True for an object whose parse attribute is the integer 42. No engine code currently uses these isinstance checks (decorator is presently decorative), but it advertises a guarantee it cannot enforce.

Independently verify: repro `class P: parse=42; source_fingerprint=0; isinstance(P(), IngestionParser)`; grep isinstance in src/. Consider counter-argument (is @runtime_checkable harmless if unused? is the "advertises a guarantee" framing fair?). Decide TRUE/PARTIAL/FALSE. Assess LOW severity.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding independently. Let me start by examining the relevant files.
The isinstance grep returned no output, confirming part of the claim. Let me read the contract files and the decorator definitions.

content.content:
1	"""Ingestion parser interface — the only adapter piece that understands the raw format.
2	
3	An adapter implements :class:`IngestionParser` to turn its native source into a canonical
4	:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.schema.Corpus`. The engine calls ``parse`` and works only
5	with the canonical result.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from pathlib import Path
11	from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable
12	
13	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
14	
15	
16	@runtime_checkable
17	class IngestionParser(Protocol):
18	    """Raw source → canonical :class:`Corpus`.
19	
20	    Implementations live in adapters. They must be deterministic: the same source bytes
21	    produce the same ``Corpus`` (so the baseline is reproducible). Implementations must not
22	    read any external-claims artifact here — claims load through a separate provider.
23	    """
24	
25	    def parse(self, source: Path) -> Corpus:
26	        """Parse ``source`` into a canonical corpus."""
27	        ...
28	
29	    def source_fingerprint(self, source: Path) -> str:
30	        """Return a stable content hash of ``source`` for provenance and determinism checks."""
31	        ...
32

1	"""External-claims source interface — optional, and quarantined from the baseline.
2	
3	If a dataset ships claims asserted by some external authority (relationships, labels, a
4	taxonomy, mappings), an adapter exposes them through :class:`ExternalClaimsSource`. The
5	engine converts each :class:`ClaimRecord` into a typed, falsifiable hypothesis and tests it
6	against the baseline — which is kept blind to these claims (Firewall A).
7	
8	This interface is *structurally separable*: the baseline pipeline never receives it, so the
9	baseline can be produced with claims withheld. Pure-discovery datasets omit it entirely.
10	Access to a claims source while claim-blindness is in force raises ``FirewallViolation``
11	(see :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.firewalls`).
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Iterable, Mapping, Protocol, runtime_checkable
18	
19	
20	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
21	class ClaimRecord:
22	    """One external, expert-asserted claim about the data — a falsifiable assertion.
23	
24	    The engine treats this as a *hypothesis source*, never as ground truth. ``subject`` and
25	    ``referents`` name the units the claim is about; ``assertion`` describes what is claimed
26	    in a typed, machine-readable way; ``provenance`` records who asserted it and where from.
27	    """
28	
29	    claim_id: str
30	    subject: str
31	    referents: tuple[str, ...]
32	    assertion: Mapping[str, Any]
33	    provenance: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
34	
35	
36	@runtime_checkable
37	class ExternalClaimsSource(Protocol):
38	    """Adapter-provided access to a dataset's external claims (adjudication mode only)."""
39	
40	    def claims(self) -> Iterable[ClaimRecord]:
41	        """Yield every external claim to be adjudicated against the blind baseline."""
42	        ...
43	
44	    def claim_fingerprint(self) -> str:
45	        """Stable content hash of the claim set, for provenance and pre-registration."""
46	        ...
47

1	"""Feature-builder interface — optional dataset-specific features.
2	
3	The baseline toolkit provides generic builders (embeddings, similarity, etc.). When a
4	dataset has features only meaningful in its own domain, an adapter supplies them through a
5	:class:`FeatureBuilder`. Output is a plain numeric :class:`FeatureMatrix` keyed by unit id,
6	so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from typing import Protocol, Sequence, runtime_checkable
13	
14	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
15	
16	
17	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
18	class FeatureMatrix:
19	    """Numeric features for a set of units. ``rows[i]`` corresponds to ``unit_ids[i]``."""
20	
21	    unit_ids: tuple[str, ...]
22	    feature_names: tuple[str, ...]
23	    rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]
24	
25	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
26	        if len(self.rows) != len(self.unit_ids):
27	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix row count must match unit_ids length")
28	        width = len(self.feature_names)
29	        if any(len(r) != width for r in self.rows):
30	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix rows must match feature_names width")
31	
32	
33	@runtime_checkable
34	class FeatureBuilder(Protocol):
35	    """Build dataset-specific numeric features from a canonical corpus."""
36	
37	    def build(self, corpus: Corpus) -> FeatureMatrix:
38	        """Compute features for (a subset of) the corpus units. Must be deterministic."""
39	        ...
40	
41	    @property
42	    def provides(self) -> Sequence[str]:
43	        """Names of the features this builder produces (for provenance)."""
44	        ...
45

15:from typing import Any, Mapping, Protocol, runtime_checkable
35:@runtime_checkable
36:class BaselineBuilder(Protocol):
39:    def build(self, corpus: Corpus, *, claim_guard: ClaimBlindGuard) -> BaselineArtifact:

content.text: All four are confirmed `@runtime_checkable` Protocols with only method members. Now let me run the repro and a broader isinstance search (including tests, to confirm no engine code uses them).

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from assay_engine.contracts.parser import IngestionParser
from assay_engine.contracts.features import FeatureBuilder
from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ExternalClaimsSource

class P:
    parse = 42
    source_fingerprint = 0

print('IngestionParser bogus:', isinstance(P(), IngestionParser))

class Q:
    build = 'nope'
    provides = None
print('FeatureBuilder bogus:', isinstance(Q(), FeatureBuilder))

# missing attr -> should be False
class R:
    parse = 42
print('IngestionParser missing source_fingerprint:', isinstance(R(), IngestionParser))
"

input.description: Search whole repo for isinstance against these protocols

content.content:
IngestionParser bogus: True
FeatureBuilder bogus: True
IngestionParser missing source_fingerprint: False

content.text: The repro confirms exactly what the finding claims. Let me also verify the broader claim that no engine code anywhere uses these isinstance checks, and check whether the protocols are used in any structural way (e.g. type annotations only).

content.text: The protocols are used only as static type annotations (in `study.py`) and exports. No `isinstance` usage anywhere in the repo. Let me confirm study.py uses them purely as annotations and check for any test that asserts isinstance behavior.

The one isinstance in tests is against `Interpretation`, an unrelated class — not one of the four adapter protocols. Let me quickly check whether `Interpretation` is also a runtime_checkable Protocol (relevant to severity/scope) and confirm the finding's count of "every adapter contract."

input.description: Inspect Interpretation class and the test

content.content:
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/fence.py:32:class Interpretation(Generic[T]):
---
        register_study("demo", lambda: StudyDefinition.discovery("demo", _StubParser(), ["q"]))
    clear_registry()


def test_fence_is_one_directional():
    m = Measurement(value=0.9, produced_by="similarity", inputs_hash="abc")
    interp = fence(m, "units are close", rationale="cosine 0.9", judged_by="operator")
    assert isinstance(interp, Interpretation)
    assert interp.basis == ("similarity",)
    # there is deliberately no inverse: Interpretation cannot become a Measurement
    assert not hasattr(interp, "as_measurement")


def test_engine_imports_no_adapter():
    """The engine must be self-contained: every submodule imports without an adapter present."""
    failures = []

content.text:
`Interpretation` is a `Generic` dataclass, not a Protocol, so that isinstance is sound and unrelated. The finding's claims are fully verified.

## Verification Summary

**Repro confirmed:** `isinstance(P(), IngestionParser)` returns `True` for `P` with `parse=42`, `source_fingerprint=0`. Same for `FeatureBuilder` with non-callable members. (Correctly returns `False` only when an attribute is entirely missing.) This is documented standard CPython behavior: `runtime_checkable` Protocol `isinstance` checks attribute *presence* only, never callability or signature.

**Grep confirmed:** No `isinstance` against any of the four adapter protocols (`IngestionParser`, `ExternalClaimsSource`, `FeatureBuilder`, `BaselineBuilder`) anywhere in `src/` or `tests/`. The only repo isinstance hit is against `Interpretation`, which is a `Generic` dataclass (not a Protocol) — sound and irrelevant.

**Counter-arguments weighed:**
- *Is it harmless if unused?* Largely, today. The protocols are consumed only as static type annotations in `study.py` (mypy-checked structural typing, which is the correct and sufficient mechanism). The decorator is presently dead weight.
- *Is "advertises a guarantee it cannot enforce" fair?* Partially. `@runtime_checkable` does advertise runtime-checkability, and that advertised capability is genuinely weak. But the finding itself concedes no code relies on it, so there is no *current* incorrect behavior — only a latent trap if someone later writes `isinstance(adapter, IngestionParser)` believing it validates the contract.

This is a real, accurately described latent issue, not a present defect. That matches PARTIAL more than TRUE_POSITIVE in spirit, but the finding's own framing already scopes it as latent/decorative, so its factual claims are all true. I assess it TRUE_POSITIVE with the caveat that impact is purely latent. LOW severity is correct (arguably even INFO/nit): no runtime impact today, isolated to the adapter onboarding boundary, easy to misuse later.

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: LOW is appropriate, leaning toward INFO/nit. Every factual claim is verified (four runtime_checkable Protocols, weak isinstance semantics reproduced, zero isinstance consumers). Real-world impact is purely latent: the only current consumers are mypy static annotations in study.py, which are sound. Risk materializes only if future code adds an isinstance check and trusts it as contract validation. No present correctness, security, or data-integrity impact.
REASONING: Read all four files — each is `@runtime_checkable class X(Protocol)` with only method/property members. Repro under the project venv returned `IngestionParser bogus: True` and `FeatureBuilder bogus: True` for objects whose members are an int/str, and `False` only when an attribute is absent — exactly the stated weakness, which is intended CPython runtime_checkable behavior (presence-only). Repo-wide grep found no isinstance against any of the four protocols; sole isinstance hit (test_contract.py:82) targets `Interpretation`, a `Generic` dataclass in methodology/fence.py, not a Protocol, so it is sound and out of scope. Protocols are otherwise referenced only as type annotations in study.py and as __init__ exports. Thus the decorator is currently decorative and the "guarantee it cannot enforce" is advertised but unexercised.
FIX_GUIDANCE: Lowest-effort and arguably best: drop `@runtime_checkable` (and the unused import) from the four contracts, since structural typing via mypy already enforces conformance at the only place it is used (study.py). If runtime validation of adapters is actually desired at the onboarding boundary, do not rely on isinstance; add an explicit validator that checks `callable(getattr(obj, name, None))` for each required method (and property presence for `provides`), called once when an adapter/StudyDefinition is registered. Keeping `@runtime_checkable` without such a validator is the trap worth removing.
